topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation felt like a deliberate jab. I stared at the embossed card, the elegant script mocking the rough edges of my memory. Dr. Elias Thorne. My old tormentor, the one who used to dismantle my confidence with a practiced, cruel smile, was now offering sessions. I traced the gold foil with a fingertip, a tightness gripping my chest. Every nerve ending screamed at the sheer audacity of it. I pictured him, years ago, belittling me by the lockers, his eyes alight with pure, uncomplicated dominance. Now, he was supposedly dissecting the very architecture of my insecurity. When I finally called, my voice came out thin, brittle. "Why are you doing this?" His reply, calm and measured, settled over me like a heavy blanket. It wasn't an apology; it was an observation. I hung up, the phone feeling slick and cold in my hand. I spent the next week avoiding his name, the memory of his presence sparking a low, internal tremor of unease.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the room, a slow, almost imperceptible tightening around my jaw. He was laughing now, a deep, resonant sound that used to make my stomach clench with a mixture of fear and defiance. It was the same laugh that had once been weaponized against me, used to amplify my shame. Now, he sat behind a mahogany desk, radiating an unnerving composure. I felt a familiar clenching in my gut, a coiled tension that refused to dissipate. When he finally looked up, his gaze wasn't mocking; it was analytical, dissecting something beneath my skin. "You seem agitated," he noted, his tone devoid of judgment, yet somehow piercing. I shifted in my seat, pulling my jacket tighter around myself. I wanted to lash out, to conjure the old, familiar sting, but the energy just pooled uselessly inside me. I just stared at the polished wood, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The first session was a study in forced neutrality. He spoke of transference and unresolved childhood dynamics, terms that felt like expertly crafted traps. I sat rigidly, my knuckles white where I gripped the armrests. Every carefully chosen word he uttered seemed to land precisely where I was most vulnerable, triggering a defensive tightening in my shoulders. I noticed the subtle way his eyes tracked my breathing, cataloging my reactions. It was the same predatory focus I remembered from schoolyard confrontations, only now it was cloaked in a veneer of professional detachment. I found myself fighting the urge to shrink, to become small and invisible. When he suggested I revisit the incident where he’d mocked my handwriting, a strange, hot wave washed over me—not anger, but a profound sense of being seen, stripped bare. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult, and managed a curt, flat response.
